title: Trump has a new Strait of Hormuz plan. The market isn’t buying it | CNN Business
text:
Source: CNN

The world is desperate for a solution to break up the oil tanker traffic jam in the Strait of Hormuz.

The Trump administration is trying to do that: It announced it had successfully guided two US vessels out of the strait Monday. But its “Project Freedom”does not appear to be the gamechangerthat’s needed to end the historic energy crisis.

At least, that’s the message from the market.

Energy prices did not tumble after President Donald Trump announced thenew US effort to “guide” shipsthrough the Strait of Hormuz.

Oil futures climbed higher above $100 a barrel and then went even higher as ships and key energy facilities in the Middle East were targeted on Monday, raising questions about the durability of the ceasefire.

And gasoline futures jumped, too, signaling pain at the pump will get worse before it gets better.

At this point, the market is betting Project Freedom will not unlock the vast amount of energy trapped in the Middle East.

The skepticism reflects a few realities:

1.)This is not an escort mission:Project Freedom is an effort to “restore freedom of navigation” in the Strait of Hormuz that will feature more than 100 land and sea-based aircraft and 15,000 service members, according to US Central Command. While Trump’s announcement shows US officials are rightly focused on reopening the Strait of Hormuz, this is not a promise to have the US military accompany vessels trying to transit the narrow waterway. In fact, a US official told CNN this will not be an escort mission.

2.)Iran says it violates the ceasefire: Officials in Iran quickly responded to Project Freedom by arguing itviolates the fragile ceasefirewith the United States. Not only that but Iran appeared to respond by resuming attacks in the region.

3.)Confidence shaken: The maritime industry has been rocked by Iran mining the Strait of Hormuz and attacking vessels that try to get through. Shipping executives are alreadyexpressing cautionabout Project Freedom and it’s unclear if tanker owners will be willing to take the risk of attempting to transit the strait.

Project Freedom falls well short of a full reopening of the Strait of Hormuz – a long process that oil analysts believe will be required tostartto get the oil sufficiently flowing through the Middle East again to bring down prices significantly. Eurasia Group warned that, unless there is “buy-in” from Iran or a major naval deployment in the region, Project Freedom will fail.

“The US plan will not substantially raise shipping volume through the strait in the near term,” consulting firm Eurasia Group wrote in a report on Monday.

That sentiment was echoed by Bjørn Højgaard, CEO of ship manager Anglo-Eastern, a ship management company.

“It takes both sides to unblock — not just one,” Højgaard said. “Either party can signal that they are willing to let certain ships through, but unless the other side accepts that in practice, it doesn’t materially change the reality on the water.”

That reality is being made even more complicated by new kinetic attacks in the region.

The US and Iranian militariestraded shotson Monday, with the US blowing up small Iranian boats in response to attacks on US assets.

Anexplosion rocked a South Korean-linked shipat the Strait of Hormuz. The cause of the explosion was unclear but the incident underscores safety concerns for vessels thinking about making the voyage.

And then amajor fire broke outat a key oil facility in the United Arab Emirates in an attack that officials there blamed on Iranian drones.

The incident damaged the Fujairah Oil Industry Zone, the end of a pipeline that is used tobypass the Strait of Hormuz.

US crude futures initially dipped Sunday night on the Project Freedom news before reversing course. West Texas Intermediate (WTI), the US benchmark, climbed as high as $107.46 a barrel on Monday and in recent trading was up 3.5% to $105 a barrel. Brent crude, the world benchmark and a key driver of gasoline prices, jumped 5% to $114 a barrel.

US gasoline futures surged on Monday as well, gaining another 4%, or 15 cents per gallon.

Retail prices at the pump skyrocketed last week and hit a fresh crisis high of $4.46 a gallon on Monday, the highest level in nearly four years.

Andy Lipow, president of Lipow Oil Associates, told CNN that gas prices willlikely hit $5 a gallonif the Strait of Hormuz remains closed for another month.

The market will be watching closely whether the flow of traffic through the Strait of Hormuz accelerates at all in the coming days.

The hope is that some of the vessels trapped in the Gulf, holding crucial supplies for the rest of the world, will finally be able to escape. But Project Freedom, at least in its early stages, appears to lack the ability to rapidly clear the significant logjam in the strait.

There are about 170 million barrels of crude oil, jet fuel, diesel and other refined products stuck in the Middle East aboard 166 tankers, according to estimates from Kpler.

“It could be a very laborious process to get laden tankers out of the Mideast Gulf – and empty ones in – given the traditional shipping lanes are not being used for fear of mines,” said Matt Smith, Kpler’s lead oil analyst.

Kpler estimates it could take up to three months once the Strait of Hormuz is fully reopen.

US officials have attempted to reassure consumers frustrated by surging gas prices.

“Help is on the way as of today,” Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said onFox Newson Monday.

Bessent noted some of the larger oil tankers stuck in the strait carry about 2 million barrels each. He hopes Project Freedom will be able to get that oil to the market soon.

“I think the market is going to be well supplied…I’m confident on the other side of this, the world’s going to be awash in oil,” Bessent said.

He also noted that OPEC recently promised to increase production. But that is a largely symbolic gesture, because the strait remains closed.

In any case, the hundreds of thousands of barrels of promised OPEC production pales in comparison to the estimated loss of around 14 million barrels per day caused by the war.

And the 170 million barrels of oil stuck in the strait, similarly is a relative drop in the bucket compared to the roughly 900 million barrels of oil that analysts estimate have been sidelined by the conflict – a sum that is growing each day the Strait of Hormuz remains closed.

CNN’s David Goldman and Kristie Lu Stout contributed to this report.
